anchor.snippet: A slide rule is a hand-operated mechanical calculator consisting of slidable rulers
Each ruler's scale has graduations labeled with precomputed outputs of various mathematical functions
a number to be multiplied on one logarithmic-scale ruler can be aligned with the start of another such ruler to sum their logarithms
Most slide rules consist of three parts
Scales may be grouped in decades , where each decade corresponds to a range of numbers that spans a ratio of 10

annotations.label: Logarithmic identities for multiplication and division

anchor.snippet: The following logarithmic identities transform the operations of multiplication and division to addition and subtraction
corresponds to shifting the top logarithmic scale by a distance of
the result of the multiplication 3×2=6 can then be read on the bottom scale under the top scale's
the answer to 7×2=14 is found by first positioning the top scale to start above the 2 of the bottom scale
to multiply 88×20 , the top scale is again positioned to start at the 2 on the bottom scale
In general, the 1 on the top is moved to a factor on the bottom, and the answer is read off the bottom where the other factor is on the top
The 2 on the top scale is placed over the 5.5 on the bottom scale
movement of the scales can be minimized by alternating divisions and multiplications
pairs of numbers that are aligned on the logarithmic scales form constant ratios
setting 7.5 on one scale over 10 on the other scale
some slide rules have other mathematical functions encoded on other auxiliary scales

annotations.label: Squaring via D and A scales

anchor.snippet: locate x on the D scale and read its square on the A scale
When several LL scales are present, use the one with x on it
To extract a cube root using a slide rule with only C/D and A/B scales
Quadratic equations of the form
The LLN scales can be used to compute and compare the cost or return on a fixed rate loan or investment
The S, T, and ST scales are used for trig functions and multiples of trig functions
sines are found by comparing the S scale with C (or D) scale
Tangents are found by comparing the T scale with the C (or D) scale for angles less than 45 degrees
For angles below 5.7 degrees, sines, tangents, and radians are approximately equal
Base-10 logarithms and exponentials are found using the L scale, which is linear
Logarithms to any other base can be calculated by reversing the procedure for calculating powers of a number
Exploits the identity that the quotient of two variables plus (or minus) one times the divisor equals their sum (or difference)
After sliding the cursor right (for addition) or left (for subtraction) and returning the slide to 0, the result can be read
Using (almost) any strictly monotonic scales , other calculations can also be made with one movement
quadratic scales can be used to solve
Reciprocal scales can be used for the equality
1.5 × 30 (which equals 45) will show the same result as 1 500 000 × 0.03
